content.text: Master Data Management for Grounding Sources

Without Master Data Management (MDM), a RAG knowledge base accumulates duplicates: the same policy document ingested from Confluence, SharePoint, and an S3 backup becomes three separate records with three separate freshness clocks and potentially three conflicting metadata sets. MDM applies the concept of a "golden record" to grounding sources — one canonical entry per unique document, with all source aliases tracked and all downstream systems referencing the same stable identifier.

attrs.alt: MDM golden record and RAG source catalog

content.text: The Golden Record Concept for RAG Sources

A golden record is the single authoritative representation of a real-world entity. In the MDM context, each unique document — regardless of how many systems store a copy — corresponds to one golden record in the source catalog. The catalog assigns a stable
golden_id
and records all source-system identifiers (aliases) that refer to the same document.
MDM Concept
RAG Equivalent
Why It Matters
Golden record
Single SourceRecord per unique document
Ensures one freshness clock, one permission set, one lineage chain
Source alias
URL / file path / SharePoint ID pointing to the same content
Allows multiple ingestion connectors to contribute to one canonical record
Duplicate detection
URL normalisation + content hashing
Prevents the same document from being indexed N times with different freshness states
Merge operation
Combine two mistakenly separate records into one golden record
Consolidates conflicting metadata and deduplicates vector store chunks
Split operation
Separate one golden record that was incorrectly merged
Restores independent freshness and permission tracking
Survivorship rules
Which source system's metadata wins on conflict
Avoids arbitrary overwrite when multiple connectors update the same field
SourceRecord — Golden Record Data Model

from __future__ import annotations
import enum
import uuid
import hashlib
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

class SourceStatus(enum.Enum):
    ACTIVE    = "active"
    RETIRED   = "retired"    # permanently removed from retrieval
    SUSPENDED = "suspended"  # temporarily blocked pending review

@dataclass
class SourceAlias:
    """A source-system-specific identifier that resolves to this golden record."""
    system: str          # e.g. "confluence", "sharepoint", "s3"
    external_id: str     # the ID in that system (URL, file key, page ID, etc.)
    added_at: datetime

@dataclass
class MergeEvent:
    merged_golden_id: str    # the ID that was absorbed
    merged_at: datetime
    merged_by: str           # user ID or service name that triggered the merge
    reason: str

@dataclass
class SourceRecord:
    golden_id: str
    canonical_title: str
    content_class: str          # e.g. "regulatory", "policy", "product_doc"
    classification_label: str   # e.g. "internal", "confidential"
    jurisdiction: str           # e.g. "EU", "US", "GLOBAL"
    permitted_apps: list[str]   # application IDs that may retrieve this source
    data_domain: str            # e.g. "compliance", "engineering", "finance"
    review_cycle_days: int      # how often a steward must review this source
    status: SourceStatus
    aliases: list[SourceAlias] = field(default_factory=list)
    merge_history: list[MergeEvent] = field(default_factory=list)
    created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc))
    last_modified_at: datetime = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc))
    content_hash: Optional[str] = None   # SHA-256 of canonical content (latest version)

    @classmethod
    def create(
        cls,
        canonical_title: str,
        content_class: str,
        classification_label: str,
        jurisdiction: str,
        permitted_apps: list[str],
        data_domain: str,
        review_cycle_days: int,
        initial_alias: Optional[SourceAlias] = None,
    ) -> "SourceRecord":
        record = cls(
            golden_id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
            canonical_title=canonical_title,
            content_class=content_class,
            classification_label=classification_label,
            jurisdiction=jurisdiction,
            permitted_apps=permitted_apps,
            data_domain=data_domain,
            review_cycle_days=review_cycle_days,
            status=SourceStatus.ACTIVE,
        )
        if initial_alias:
            record.aliases.append(initial_alias)
        return record

    def add_alias(self, system: str, external_id: str) -> None:
        existing = {(a.system, a.external_id) for a in self.aliases}
        if (system, external_id) not in existing:
            self.aliases.append(SourceAlias(system, external_id, datetime.now(timezone.utc)))

    def update_content_hash(self, raw_text: str) -> None:
        self.content_hash = hashlib.sha256(raw_text.encode()).hexdigest()
        self.last_modified_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
Deduplication Strategy
Deduplication runs when a new document is submitted for ingestion. It first tries to match by URL/ID (fast path), then falls back to content hashing (catches mirrors and reformatted copies).
Merge and Split Operations

When two golden records are found to describe the same document, a merge collapses them into one. The absorbed record's aliases are transferred to the survivor; its chunks in the vector store are re-tagged with the survivor's
golden_id
. Splits are the reverse: a single golden record is replaced by two, with chunks reassigned based on their source alias.

from __future__ import annotations
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Callable

class SourceCatalog:
    def __init__(self, store: dict) -> None:
        self._store: dict[str, SourceRecord] = store   # golden_id → SourceRecord

    def find_by_alias(self, system: str, external_id: str) -> Optional[SourceRecord]:
        for record in self._store.values():
            for alias in record.aliases:
                if alias.system == system and alias.external_id == external_id:
                    return record
        return None

    def find_by_content_hash(self, content_hash: str) -> Optional[SourceRecord]:
        for record in self._store.values():
            if record.content_hash == content_hash:
                return record
        return None

    def save(self, record: SourceRecord) -> None:
        self._store[record.golden_id] = record

    def merge(
        self,
        survivor_id: str,
        absorbed_id: str,
        merged_by: str,
        reason: str,
        retag_chunks: Callable[[str, str], None],  # (old_golden_id, new_golden_id)
    ) -> SourceRecord:
        """Merge absorbed_id into survivor_id. Transfer aliases; retag vector chunks."""
        survivor = self._store[survivor_id]
        absorbed = self._store[absorbed_id]

        for alias in absorbed.aliases:
            survivor.add_alias(alias.system, alias.external_id)

        survivor.merge_history.append(MergeEvent(
            merged_golden_id=absorbed_id,
            merged_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            merged_by=merged_by,
            reason=reason,
        ))

        retag_chunks(absorbed_id, survivor_id)

        absorbed.status = SourceStatus.RETIRED
        self.save(survivor)
        self.save(absorbed)
        return survivor

    def split(
        self,
        original_id: str,
        aliases_for_new: list[tuple[str, str]],  # (system, external_id) to move
        new_record_kwargs: dict,
        split_by: str,
        reason: str,
        retag_chunks: Callable[[str, str, list[str]], None],
    ) -> tuple[SourceRecord, SourceRecord]:
        """Create a new golden record from a subset of aliases from original_id."""
        original = self._store[original_id]
        new_record = SourceRecord.create(**new_record_kwargs)

        aliases_to_move = {(s, e) for s, e in aliases_for_new}
        original.aliases = [
            a for a in original.aliases
            if (a.system, a.external_id) not in aliases_to_move
        ]
        for system, external_id in aliases_for_new:
            new_record.add_alias(system, external_id)

        external_ids = [e for _, e in aliases_for_new]
        retag_chunks(original_id, new_record.golden_id, external_ids)

        self.save(original)
        self.save(new_record)
        return original, new_record
Survivorship Rules for Metadata Conflicts
When multiple connectors contribute to the same golden record, their metadata may disagree. Survivorship rules define which value wins without requiring manual arbitration on every conflict.
Field
Survivorship Rule
Rationale
content_class
Most restrictive value wins
A document classified as 'regulatory' by one system should not be downgraded to 'product_doc' by another
classification_label
Most restrictive wins (confidential > internal > public)
Same principle; never silently downgrade sensitivity
permitted_apps
Intersection of all connector values
A document available to app X only in one system should not become available to all apps
canonical_title
Source system priority order (Confluence > SharePoint > S3)
Prefer the authoritative content management system
source_updated_at
Most recent value wins
Use the latest known modification timestamp
jurisdiction
Explicit value wins over 'GLOBAL' default
A connector that knows the jurisdiction is more precise than a fallback
Reference Data Management

Reference data are the shared lookup tables that all downstream systems read: content class taxonomy, jurisdiction codes, permitted app registry, and classification labels. These must be governed as first-class MDM entities because every SourceRecord and every chunk in the vector store depends on them being consistent.
content_class taxonomy.

A controlled vocabulary (e.g. regulatory / policy / product_doc / research / market_data). Additions require steward approval; removals must migrate all existing records to a replacement class before the old value is retired.
permitted_app registry.
A master list of application IDs that may retrieve from the knowledge base, along with the max classification level each application may access. The retriever rejects app IDs not in this registry.
jurisdiction codes.
A controlled list (ISO 3166 country codes + custom regions like EU, APAC, GLOBAL). Used by the residency gate and data lineage metadata.
classification labels.
Mapped to the organisation's data classification scheme. Changes to this mapping propagate to every chunk in the vector store — require a migration job, not an in-place update.
Reference data changes are breaking changes.

Renaming a content_class value or removing a permitted_app without migrating chunks leaves the vector store in an inconsistent state. Treat reference data updates with the same discipline as database schema migrations: backwards-compatible additions first, then migration, then removal.
Integration with the Ingestion Pipeline

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class IngestionRequest:
    system: str
    external_id: str
    raw_text: str
    proposed_metadata: dict

def mdm_aware_ingest(
    request: IngestionRequest,
    deduplicator: SourceDeduplicator,
    catalog: SourceCatalog,
    apply_survivorship: Callable[[SourceRecord, dict], SourceRecord],
    chunk_and_index: Callable[[str, str, dict], None],  # (golden_id, text, metadata)
) -> str:
    """Ingest a document via MDM: dedup, create or update golden record, index chunks."""
    result = deduplicator.check(
        request.system, request.external_id, request.raw_text
    )

    if result.is_duplicate and result.existing_golden_id:
        # Existing golden record — apply survivorship to update metadata
        record = catalog.find_by_alias(request.system, request.external_id)
        if record is None:
            record = catalog._store[result.existing_golden_id]
        record = apply_survivorship(record, request.proposed_metadata)
        record.update_content_hash(request.raw_text)
        catalog.save(record)
    else:
        # New document — create golden record
        alias = SourceAlias(
            system=request.system,
            external_id=request.external_id,
            added_at=__import__("datetime").datetime.now(__import__("datetime").timezone.utc),
        )
        record = SourceRecord.create(
            canonical_title=request.proposed_metadata.get("title", request.external_id),
            content_class=request.proposed_metadata["content_class"],
            classification_label=request.proposed_metadata["classification_label"],
            jurisdiction=request.proposed_metadata.get("jurisdiction", "GLOBAL"),
            permitted_apps=request.proposed_metadata.get("permitted_apps", []),
            data_domain=request.proposed_metadata.get("data_domain", "general"),
            review_cycle_days=int(request.proposed_metadata.get("review_cycle_days", 90)),
            initial_alias=alias,
        )
        record.update_content_hash(request.raw_text)
        catalog.save(record)

    chunk_and_index(record.golden_id, request.raw_text, {
        "golden_id": record.golden_id,
        "content_class": record.content_class,
        "classification_label": record.classification_label,
        "permitted_apps": record.permitted_apps,
        "jurisdiction": record.jurisdiction,
    })
    return record.golden_id
Summary

Applying MDM to RAG grounding sources prevents duplicate ingestion, enforces consistent metadata across all connectors, and provides the stable golden identifiers that freshness scanners, quality checks, lineage trackers, and steward dashboards all require. The three core mechanisms — deduplication by alias and content hash, merge/split operations with audit trails, and survivorship rules for metadata conflicts — ensure that the knowledge base maintains a single source of truth for each document regardless of how many systems store a copy.
